era_context: The Late Bronze Age remains stable in its final century before systemic collapse. Egypt under Ramesses II is at the height of its power. The Hittite Empire is consolidating after internal conflicts. Mycenaean Greece continues to prosper. The Levant is divided between Egyptian and Hittite spheres of influence. In China, the Shang dynasty rules the Yellow River valley.

events.title: Ramesses II's reign at its height
events.description: Ramesses II, one of Egypt's greatest pharaohs, is in the middle of his long and prosperous reign (c. 1279–1213 BCE). He has consolidated power, defeated the Hittites at Kadesh, and established a peace treaty with the Hittite Empire. His building projects are monumental, including the temples at Abu Simbel, Karnak, and Luxor. Egypt is economically prosperous and militarily powerful.

sources.name: Egyptian temple inscriptions and reliefs; administrative papyri

events.certainty_note: Ramesses II's reign is well-documented through contemporary inscriptions and archaeological evidence. His regnal dates are established with high confidence.

events.title: Hittite consolidation under Hattusili III
events.description: Hattusili III consolidates his rule over the Hittite Empire, having overcome internal rivals and established the peace treaty with Egypt. He strengthens the empire's administrative structure and maintains diplomatic relations with neighboring powers. However, the empire faces mounting external pressures from the Sea Peoples and internal instability that will eventually lead to its collapse.

sources.name: Hittite cuneiform texts and royal inscriptions from Boğazköy

events.certainty_note: Hittite chronology is debated; regnal dates vary by ±10–15 years. Hattusili III's rule is attested, but the precise dating of 1262 BCE is uncertain.

events.title: Mycenaean palatial administration and Linear B record-keeping
events.description: The Mycenaean palaces at Mycenae, Tiryns, and Pylos continue to function as administrative centers. Linear B script is used to record administrative and economic information, including inventories of goods, personnel, and religious offerings. These records provide insight into the complex bureaucratic organization of Mycenaean society, though the palaces are increasingly fortified, suggesting growing instability.

sources.name: Linear B tablets from Mycenaean palace sites

events.certainty_note: Linear B tablets are contemporary records. The dating is based on pottery typology and stratigraphic context, placing them in the LH IIIB period (c. 1300–1200 BCE).

events.title: Shang dynasty bronze production and ritual practices
events.description: The Shang dynasty continues to produce some of the finest bronze vessels of the ancient world, used in ritual and ceremonial contexts. Oracle bone divination remains central to Shang governance and religious practice. The Shang court maintains control over the Yellow River valley through a combination of military force, ritual authority, and administrative networks.

sources.name: Bronze vessels and oracle bone inscriptions

events.certainty_note: Shang bronze production and oracle bone divination are well-attested. The specific dating of 1262 BCE is based on traditional chronology, which is debated.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the chronology of the Hittite Empire and the dating of Hattusili III's reign remain debated among scholars.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1262 BCE is similar to that for 1260–1261 BCE. Egyptian and Hittite sources dominate the written record, while Mycenaean and Chinese evidence is primarily archaeological or fragmentary. Large regions of the world remain undocumented.

graph_edges.note: Ramesses II's reign continues; Egyptian-Hittite peace treaty is maintained
